title: Andrew Reese -Hiring Manger Interview | Founding Account Executive
meeting_title: Andrew Reese -Hiring Manger Interview | Founding Account Executive

transcript.text: I didn't realize it was going to work like this.
This is awkward.
I was just talking about a messy deal.
And so all that happened, tried to just, you know, repair that trust by being transparent and like honoring what we originally discussed.
And then we ultimately closed a $1.4 million deal with them where it gave everybody across the company access.
Nice.
I feel like I've seen that story before.
Like the 11th hour.
Too many cooks in the kitchen.
feel like Kyle's probably heard me say that, but too many cooks in the kitchen on deals.
It's just like disaster.
Yeah, it is.
The deal room is just blowing up.
Yeah.
Everyone's like, let me get, let me get in there.
I can do it.
Exactly.
Okay.
I didn't even know that this was going to happen.
We're all in the same Zoom meeting.
I'm going to tell Tucker this is not, this is too weird, but it was super nice to meet you.
And I will let, I'm going to drop and let you and Kyle chat.
Yeah.
And then we'll be in touch later.
That's good.
Nice to meet you, Bridget.
You too.
I hope I don't end the meeting.
Wait, Kyle, let me make you the host.
Yeah, I think you should have an option as you leave to transfer it.
Okay, hopefully I don't end this.
Bye.
Bye.
Hey, Kyle, how's it going?
Good, man.
You're seeing our scrappiness and real time here.
I love it.
I miss it.
It reminds me of my early times at Miro where we were just making stuff up as we went.
It's the best time, in my opinion.
I mean, it's been wild, to be honest.
Like, when I first was here, we didn't even, like, have a recruiting team.
And we'd have all these, like, inbound.
And we did a lot of different things, leveraging workflows that our CTO built.
And we were basically, like, I was a little hesitant of how we approached it at first because, like, it felt maybe too impersonal.
And I know there's some, like, controversy around, like, where AI sits around in interviews and how you leverage it.
But...
So actually talking to like a lot of people we hired, they were like, I actually like the process.
And it was like very upfront.
Our kind of pitch to how we thought about it was like, you know, we asked for a little bit more time up front, but it hopefully means that total process is a little bit smaller.
But anyways, long story short, like we've just done so many like weird little scrappy things.
Now we have recruiters, which is amazing.
But yeah, still working out some of the things of like, how do we do multi, like, you know, you know, we have more people evaluating a candidate and how does that work?
And because, you know, some of these things, I hired a lot of people on the delivery side.
And it was usually just like, all right, you went through the AI stuff.
We're talking, love the conversation, ready to make you an offer.
And you're like, what?
I'm like, yeah, it's a weird process for sure.
So we're evolving.
Yeah, feel like people always appreciate genuine over polished.
And I guess as long as it's like personal and real.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I mean, it was a necessity for
To survive and to grow fast.
You know, demand has oftentimes not been one of our challenges.
And it's one of the probably exciting things for folks in a role like this where like, I'm not sure if you got much background on me, but like now I'm leading on the sales side.
But previously I was leading the client services side of the business.
Just about a year ago, I actually signed up to be the first customer.
So I've had this like interesting journey of kind of seeing the different like sides and growth of this company.
But anyways, when I was on the client services side, I was more like, guys, you're killing me with how much you're closing right now.
Like I am like trying to hire so fast.
I'm doing three kickoffs a week.
And now I'm like, I'm now trying to, you know, be on that other side of it and, you know, do it smartly, of course, and set the team up for success.
And that's the whole goal.
But yeah, it's been a good problem to have.
Still a problem.
Still But, you know, you're going to have a problem.
So it certainly needs to be thought of and approach in the right way if it's going to be healthy, like consistent growth, that we're going to be sticky.
And we've learned so much along the way of, you know, we had certain assumptions, we felt relatively confident, and that didn't work.
And, you know, we just keep testing and adapting.
But, yeah, you know, inbound has generally not been our problem, which, you know, is kind of a dream for a lot of AEs out there.
My wife actually is, you know, been in sales a long time.
It's funny, like I've usually been the marketer, she's the salesperson, and she would kill to have the amount of inbound that we have here.
So you may get like a couple and, you know, a quarter or two that's worthwhile, you know, so.
But anyways, great to connect.
Great to meet you.
I think you're based in Austin?
Yeah, great to meet you as well.
I'm in Austin.
Cool, cool.
I love Austin.
It's been a
Awesome, I've been there.
My cousin was just talking about moving there, and I was singing its praises.
I think he's a musician, so I feel like it's a great fit for the culture.
Yeah, exactly.
Yeah, I love it here.
It gets a little hot during the summer, but aside from that, it's not bad.
Are you in the Bay Area?
Sorry?
Are you in the Bay Area?
I am, yeah.
I'm in Oakland.
So I grew up in the East Coast.
I grew up in New Hampshire.
I went to school and started my career on Rhode Island.
And I've been in the Bay for 13 years now, so very much home.
I love the East Bay especially, and so much to my parents' chagrin that are still in New Hampshire.
But now that I have a two-and-a-half-year-old, they make that flight a lot more frequently than they used to.
They care more about coming to see him than me, apparently.
I know exactly what mean.
I'm a four-year-old, and same thing.
And my parents, when they call, I just hand the phone to him because they don't even care to talk to me.

So I'm just like, I'm just like the caregiver of their favorite person now, which yeah, we always talk via FaceTime and so my son will just like take the phone away and he'll just walk around the house talking to them and showing them things and that's become the routine now.
But yeah, that's cool.
Well, I know we have limited time.
I could always talk about my kid all day.

But yeah, we'd love to just, you know, learn a little bit more about your experience, you know, things like selling into marketers, obviously, you know, being a marketer myself, I have a keen interest and part of like, you know, the inspiration of kind of going a somewhat unconventional path of like, you know, someone who's like, you know, aspects of sales, roles and varying stops back when I was agency side, it's part of just like what comes with leading teams typically in some capacity.
But, you know, part of the inspiration here was that I should be
I've been in the role, I've been on all sides of this relationship, hopefully that brings some trust to prospects, and that's been kind of the angle we've been exploring.
But also, it's not like super scalable, right?

And so we're at this phase where we need to, how do we move beyond pure founder-led sales with Marcel, who's great at it, leverage folks like myself, but even what I'm finding is, you know, needing support and capacity to like ultimately get to the goals we need to.
So as I'm sure with shared, like experience selling into marketers and understanding them, we view as important.
I'm sure there's aspects we can train, but our current assumption is like those that have, you know, some things they can lean on and already experience there would be of great help for us.
And so that was one of the core things I just wanted to cover with you today.
Thank you.
So, you know, certainly it looks like, you know, Miro, Canva certainly have some experience there.

Before we kind of jump into like some like specific examples, I guess one of the curiosities I had, when you're selling into those marketers typically, like what are the key objectives typically that are on their mind?
And like, where are the parallels to like the objectives maybe we would be thinking about when selling into marketers from your experience?
Yeah, good question.
You mean from my experience, like what, when I've sold to marketers?
Correct.
Yeah.

Like I assume there's some overlap and I have, I'm curious if there's some differences of like, you know, you know, what ultimately they're trying to achieve that you have to be mindful of and angling that deal.
Yeah, so the, the roles I've sold into marketers the most, Canva is number one.
And then Miro, I sold to a lot of marketers.
Our main persona at Miro were product development teams, but then after that, marketing teams, because we helped just streamline the product development life cycle.
And then the next step was launching those products.

And so working with Mike Marketing to take all that work, collaborate with the work that had been done by the product development team, and then taking that and packaging it into the different work streams there for life cycle marketing or content marketing or product marketing.
And so at Miro, selling to program managers, usually in larger companies, or someone from the PMO that was supporting on the marketing side.

So there might be program managers or MarTech or marketing operations that would usually be our main technical buyer, but then working with stakeholders that were in content marketing, product marketing.
Marketing, lifecycle marketing, and then, you know, sometimes just various other, you know, I guess, buzzy function names, depending on the company.
mean, it can vary a lot, as I've seen many times.
Yeah.
Growth, blah, blah, blah.
Yeah, exactly.
Growth.
There's like, yeah, growth, marketing, product growth, which is kind of all kinds of interchangeable buzzwords, but, or titles.
But mainly selling to those titles to help, you know, streamline the process of collaborating and aligning on all the work to launch these campaigns.
And then connecting the dots or bridging the gap between product and marketing so that they could do that effectively.
And, you know, CMOs were often like the budget holder for it.
And then Canva, that was our primary persona or marketers is, as you know, it was...
On brand and content creation, and it's more or less the same personas, but then brand and creative operations were a big part of that too.

So like creative operations, like the, you know, head of creative or, you know, I guess the different variations of that title would often be, you know, they're the ones that were making decisions on tools that would actually amplify their content creation strategy.

And Canva, the whole spiel was we could help amplify the creation of branded content in a fraction of the time it takes with other tools like Adobe, because you could spin up, you know, with our AI focus features, you can spin up, you know, content, variations of content, localize it really quickly.
You can, you know, take something that's meant for Facebook, and then quickly do the magic resize, put it for, for, you know, LinkedIn, and we just have.
All these features that help streamline the content creation process.
So usually it's creative operations.
They were a pretty common title, but CMOs were generally involved, MarTech, a lot of the same titles from Miro.

And to answer your part of the question about where you think the alignment or overlap is with growthx, I feel like a lot of those, you know, the people at content marketing, creative operations, the technical support roles like program managers and MarTech.
I think all those functions would be really involved in trying to figure out how to leverage AI to streamline the content creation process.
Not only streamline, but amplify the reach and, you know, do like the, you know, SEO optimization, the content creation, every aspect of it.
I think it touches a few different teams, but more or less.
Same personas.
Yeah.
it sound like it's.
Yeah, no, I can certainly see some overlap.
My assumption whether right or wrong would be, and this kind of leads to an additional question I had, was like, I guess it's kind of a few part question.
So bear with me for a second as I kind of like organize my thoughts.
That's, you know, one, I would imagine it depends on the person you're kind of starting that conversation with and how that's progressing.
Like, I'm not sure for you if that oftentimes started with some of those folks you mentioned in, like, content ops, creative ops, whatever.
And then figuring out how do you bring the CMO in the mix.
For us, I would say more often than not, we're actually starting at that level.
It's not universally true.
And so that somewhat frames the conversation a little bit.
So I'm curious to understand a little bit of, like, your experience there.
But the other is, like, in terms of how they would.
Think about the impact, the outcomes from the work we have.
And I think there's probably some overlap in there at the end of the day, enabling things like paid or organic programs, et cetera.
And so for us, ultimately, they want us to help them drive more revenue.
And so we have to make sure they feel confident, like I have X dollars to put towards this.
Can this generate more revenue for this than something else?
And so, yeah, I'm curious to understand like the parallels of like how that, you know, how did that buying center you typically work with?
Like what was the progression of that at a Canva, a Miro?
And then like what were some of like the key objections you typically were having to tackle throughout that process?
Sure.
Yeah.
So just to clarify, like kind of what level I entered in in that dynamic.
All right.
So.
So.
All right.

We would often, there's times that we went from the groundswell, working with practitioners first, because it's usually easier to get engaged with someone that's not the target of every salesperson out there.

So there are times, I'd often, I'd go all the way up the chain, but usually, I'd actually usually try to talk to the practitioners and the managing the workflows day to day that I was trying to address.
So I could really do thorough discovery, understand their current state, their current workflows, the challenges they're facing, and then what negative consequences is causing for their business.
And then understand, like, from their perspective, what would be, like, how, what would be the best way to improve that?
Like, what was their wish list of what they'd like to see to improve it?
And then, you know, what kind of goals they have on improving, like, what that means for the business.
And then I'd go and take that story to go, then reach out to decision makers saying, hey, you know, I've.
I talked to some people on your team.
I realized that with these certain workflows, these are the biggest challenges today and it's causing these issues.
It seems like the team wants to do this.
I'd love to show you how our product or our service can really help deliver that value that you're looking for.
And so it helped, you know, first off, really engage them.

And then second, frame the conversation to where, you know, they came in just really aligned and interested in what we had to offer and establishing myself more of a, as a consultant, someone that they'd hire McKinsey or BCG to go do all this research for them.
But, you know, they're ready to help them through the process.
And so, you know, that's often how it started is, you know, doing kind of insight gathering from the practitioners and those functions, and then taking it to senior decision makers.
But sometimes, you know, it just, I also want to cut straight.
You know, if there was just a good in there, like, you know, Cliff at Canva, he's the COO, he has, you know, people know him.
And so there were times where a big company where it was just, I probably wouldn't be able to actually get all that insight and research done in the time that Canva wanted to land that logo.
I just have, you know, ghostwrite an email for Cliff to reach out.
And then usually the CMO, if we don't talk to them, then they would connect us with someone on their team and give us a hall pass to go around.
So we took both broach, like bottom up and then top down.
Yeah, makes sense.
What was a typical deal cycle for you in terms of time at both stops?
So they're both enterprise.
At Canva, I was one of the first strategic reps.
So it was all Fortune 500 accounts.
At Miro, it was 1,000 plus employees.
So it was enterprise.
A fair amount of Fortune 500 in there.
so it was usually like between six to 12 months, I'd say usually nine to 12 for strategic deals, you know, closer to six for those velocity deals.
Yep.
And then, you know, once we landed, it would start becoming like we had to land and expand motion where we just tried to get our foot in the door with, you know, whatever.
We could get an enterprise contract just moving the fastest and then we expand there.
And then there was like higher velocity once we had a relationship.
Got it.
Got it.
Yeah, it's one of the things for us that's kind of interesting.
I'm not sure if I'm trying to call back to the conversation with Tucker if he shared, but our deals tend to move quite fast.
There's some that can close in two calls like we just had one today.
I'm quite confident we'll close maybe in pre-calls just to like review the proposal.
And, you know, I just talked with this CMO.
I met with him.
Monday of last week, we just had a call today, went really well, he seemed super interested, hopefully I've already sent him the SoW, you know, hopefully we can close by like Monday next week.

And so there's some that move really, really fast, you know, maybe a month ish, you know, we're certainly finding as we get into like enterprise, like just those procurement teams, oh my god, they move so damn slow.
And, you know, we're closing a deal with like outreach for a while now.
And that could have easily closed a while ago, but we're just stuck in their procurement queue.
And it's going to be like 30 days.
But anyways, point being, I'm curious how you would think about like, you know, we're obviously in early days of refinement of our process here.

How would you typically think about ensuring you're going through and going, going through those necessary steps and not, you know, I worry sometimes about artificially lengthening our sales process, but also being mindful of like, our sales process is naturally going to happen.
We like lengthen in varying ways as we go up market more, as we introduce outbound more.
So I'm curious if you had any like similar, you know, journeys of like how, you know, that sales process may have like adapted.
Because it sounds like in some of these stops, you know, I think you were, am remembering correctly, you were founding AE at one of these?
Or am I?
Yeah, founding AE at Miro.

So I'm curious if there's any parallels there, and how you would think about, you know, that journey of helping us like, it's almost kind of a superpower, but also like, like a classic thing, like it can become a weakness if we don't handle it in the right way.
So yeah, that's a really good question.
And for me, I found that is there was always like those velocity deals, especially during COVID at Miro.
Like there's one, one time I closed 150k deal in less than two months.
And I was shocked that, you know, that we were able to get a, you know, 3000 person company to buy that quickly, but it was great.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I don't think anybody thought, like, it was just crazy stuff was happening where we're able to do high-velocity, high-value deals.
So I do love when that's possible, and I never want to be the one slowing a deal down.
And so I learned during my time at Miro just to bucket, identify as early as you can.
Like, when you are doing discovery, obviously, like, Bant is a very basic part of that, like, where you figure out what they're, you know, if they have budget for it.
They have authority, and, like, this is ready to be signed off on.
You know, the need and timeline all match up.

And then use that to kind of bucket into near-term deals, like we're just going to close in the next, you know, whatever the kind of buckets would be for growthx, like 30 days or less, you know, or maybe 30 to 90 days in the 90 days plus.
And then I'd prioritize based off of my activities on those accounts that, you know, we had that opportunity to close quickly.

While making sure that I was still keeping track of the next steps, and after I went through all those tasks on a daily or weekly basis, then working on the more strategic deals where it was going to be a lot of going back and forth, but it was still important to keep the ball in their court and never let it get stuck in line.
Totally.
Yeah, it makes sense.
Well, I realize we only have eight minutes left.
I went by fast.
I want to make sure you had time for questions as well.
Sure.
I guess, first off, do you have any concerns after talking to me or anything that needs clarification?
Concerns?
No.

I think I would assume, whether right or wrong, there's probably a little bit of a learning curve on how to position something that's going to be very channel-centric to the degree of what our offering is.
I can see parallels to where Canva and Mirror would be, because that's still touching channels in it.
But probably a little bit closer to them being able to think about the outputs and how that connects to ROI.
Admittedly, we are not necessarily telling this very clear, like, we're not doing, like, this is going to be your exact return assumption or anything like that.

But it does, it is a bit of a mindset of just, like, being able to appreciate, like, kind of where they're coming from and how we can, like, tell the story around why they should be so excited to work with us in varying ways.
But I think there's likely enough parallels where, like, that can be learned a bit, trained.
So, you know, I think, you know, your background of, like, jumping in early, selling to marketers, definitely intriguing for me.

And, like, you know, I think just generally, like, people who are going to be a good fit for this are going to be those that are, like, I don't expect this to be, like, you know, super dialed in process-wise.
I'm excited about that.
I'm excited.

To form it, you know, there's going to be some curveballs along the way, but with that comes a lot of impact opportunity to just for moldability, to being able to like, just like tangibly be able to close a lot of deals fast.
And, you know, to me, I think it's an exciting opportunity.

You know, my assumption going into this, you're transparently the first person I've at least talked to in this phase, is like, just those that, you know, it's not like we have like BDRs here, like we're having to own like and wear many, many different hats.

And, you know, that's not going to be a fit for some, but for those that, you know, like that kind of phase and moldability and are comfortable being in the ambiguity, messy middle, you know, I think it's quite exciting.
So, you know, your background seems to fit that profile.
So, yeah.
Yeah, well, thanks for the insight.
And yeah, I really like the building and the scaling part.
I think it's fun when you get to do both because you can actually get to...
Be like, you know, I've built that when things are going well and you really get to make an impact.
And it's always fun when you have enough space, like room and autonomy to figure things out and do something that you probably wouldn't be able to do elsewhere.
And, you know, I like learning a lot, too, when we get to do more than just wearing one hat, it's just rewarding.
So definitely, I'm one of the crazy people that runs towards the ambiguity and the craziness of early stage, which is why I've done it multiple times.
But it seems, I mean, growthx seems like a really cool product and I've been excited as I've learned about it after, you know, Tucker reaching out.
So, I guess my, so question for you is, what do you think the biggest focus is or should be for growthx over the next six to 12 months?
Oh, man.
Not to put you on the spot.
It can go so many ways.
You know, obviously, it's not that we're not talking about this in plenty.
It's just we have no shortage of opportunities and directions.

And, you know, I think naturally for an earlier stage, you know, you know, for example, like early on when I when I signed up to be the first customer, like part of how I discovered all this was like Marcel was doing workshops last year.
And, you know, you still see Moe Decram.
And it was a very like SEO centric focus workshop and how to apply these workflows.

But while I was very excited about that, and I could see the opportunity, I was like, man, you so many other things we can be doing, you know, whether it's like different things across the website that touch other channels, paid sales enablement, like, you know, got the gears turning.
Early on, we had to have like, you know, some focus.
And so we can't just be like too broad, we have to here Thank
Make sure we're doing something well.

And, you know, we've tried to refine, you know, approach into, you know, specifically focusing on building content engines and, you know, really starting out with like a special, like heavy focus on editorial, longer form content and how that can open up a variety of paths.
And then you can start to figure out those other like, you know, opportunities.

And so that in itself, like where that all goes, those other opportunities, a lot of tangible effects on like what we would build and like how that works and what is the team construction to support that.
And we were still going through a lot of that learning.
You know, what is most exciting to me, I don't know if this was shared, but this is the first official week.
We are off air ops as a part of our, as a part of our motion.
And this was kind of a progressive thing.
First, it started with us building the step-by-step workflows in air ops and, you know, doing a lot of championing, partnering with them to develop that.
Then over time, you know, we just found different things that were creating friction.
So we started to.
Track some of those workflows and ping via API to things we were building ourselves.
And then that just opened up platform opportunities for us to just build our own thing.
And this is the week we've officially launched that internally.
It's still got some bugs to work out.
We're not going to open up.
But there's a password, and I was just talking to a deal this morning.
was actually the one I was talking about earlier that's going to move pretty fast.
One of the things that's on his mind is he used to build a narrow ops.
And he wants to get to a point where he can touch and feel and run things at some point.
And we are a matter of a month or so away from that being a thing.
And so as we do that, it just opens up a lot of different paths for where does the services sit alongside of that?
And how does this inform some of the other things that we do from a Top of Funnels perspective on how we educate?
And, you know, we see a lot of opportunity to build community and...
You know, do varying trainings.
And when you have your own platform that like presents a lot of opportunity for you to do that, right?
It's a little bit challenging in some of the phases we just went through because some of that like abstracted away.
And so it's like, what are people going to do with this?
They don't, they weren't going to be able to take and build with this the way we built it.
It's too abstracted for them.
And so now we have a platform, you know, that could be something that fits into a part of this mix in some really, really interesting ways.
And I think we're just taking an approach that I just some of the things I saw this week, I'm like, damn, that's super cool.
I can't wait.
And like, it feels like we're just scratching the surface.
And so I'm not 100% answering your question there.
But like that in like, you know, in itself opens up these different paths of what then features do you prioritize next?
Do you start doing things where, you know, you may have apps that you have as like lead magnet type things, value add type things that like it's separate, like for example, example,

We built a pretty cool comprehensive fact checker that's really relevant to a lot of what we do because at the end of the day, people are going to have a bit of a hesitation around hallucinations related to LLMs and the quality of content.
then sometimes we talk with brands that are in the finance or medical space, and we have a variety of those where there needs to be some sort of legal or medical review.
And how do we approach that?
Are you guys going to hire a professional?
What does that cost?
And, you know, we just closed one of these recently, and my pitch to them was, like, it's cost prohibitive for us to, like, hire a doctor to review every piece of content that we create for you.
Like, we can maybe figure out the dollars there, but, like, it's going to be crazy expensive.
It's not going to make sense.
But our fact checker can, they have people internally, it's like, how do we reduce the burden of them doing that?
And this fact checker, like, it's really cool.
parses out, like, you know, the different, like, passwords.
And a piece of content, and it basically looks at them and chunks them and says, what kind of research can I find that validates this statement, essentially?
And you can point that in different directions.
could be public sources, or you could, you know, narrow the aperture towards, like, particular resources and say, like, you know, treat this as your source tree.
And it could be adapted for, you know, what makes sense for a different brand.
And that in itself could be a standalone app that we do.
And we literally, you know, we spun up a domain.
We kind of have that stood up.
Like, let's see what we do with it.
We probably market it one day.
But, like, stuff like that could, like, open up.
It's, like, different things we do that's super interesting just to show off the work, how we think, be, in a sense, a lead magnet that then could, like, feed the top of funnel for us.
And so, you know, I think we're still kind of, like, figuring out some of that and prioritizing some.
We just got through this major, like, heads-down time of, like, let's get the platform live.
Let's get.
You know, some of the key features live on that, that stress test to figure out some of the bugs.

And I think we're at a point of like now debating a little bit of like what comes next, because there's definitely going to be some things where like, damn, like these five things we really want to do so badly, but we can only pick three.
For example, like what are those three?
Yeah.
So it's an exciting time.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's exciting.
Yeah.
It definitely seems like there's a bunch of adjacent workflows that every time you can start building functionality for them as well, which is cool.

And I guess the platform launch, is that going to be like, you know, Aerov slash writer slash like Jasper competitor, but with the service piece built in, I guess, not just the platform, it's the whole package.
Yeah.
think it could go a few different directions.
And to be honest, I'm not sure we've like fully like committed to like one or the other.
A lot of what is.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview with Andrew Reese for a Founding Account Executive role at growthx.ai](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Andrew has relevant experience selling to marketers at Miro and Canva, with a focus on enterprise deals](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=531.0)
  - [GrowthX's unique model combines AI-powered content creation with high-quality strategic services](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=782.0)
  - [The company is in an exciting growth phase, transitioning from founder-led sales to building out the sales team](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1239.0)
  - [GrowthX recently launched their own platform, opening up new opportunities for product development and go-to-market strategies](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1913.0)

## Topics

### Andrew's Background and Experience

  - [Founding AE at Miro, selling to enterprise accounts (1000+ employees, Fortune 500)](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1222.0)
  - [Strategic AE at Canva, focusing on Fortune 500 accounts](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1052.0)
  - [Typical deal cycles: 6-12 months for enterprise, with 9-12 months for strategic deals](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1066.0)
  - [Experience selling to various marketing roles: CMOs, creative operations, content marketing, product marketing, MarTech](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=531.0)

### GrowthX's Current State and Challenges

  - [Transitioning from founder-led sales to building out the sales team](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1239.0)
  - [High inbound interest, but need to scale sales capacity](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1126.0)
  - [Recently launched their own platform, moving away from Airtable](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1913.0)
  - [Balancing services with potential self-serve options in the future](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1200.0)
  - [Rapid deal cycles (some closing in 2 calls) compared to typical enterprise sales](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1126.0)

### GrowthX's Unique Value Proposition

  - [Combination of AI-powered content creation and high-quality strategic services](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=782.0)
  - [Experienced team providing insights across multiple accounts](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1419.0)
  - [Ability to create custom, experiential content (e.g., Vappy use case with interactive AI agents)](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=2760.0)
  - [Focus on driving tangible business outcomes for clients](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1493.0)

### Market Dynamics and Opportunities

  - [Emerging field of "generative engine optimization" (GEO/AISEO)](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=2263.0)
  - [Partnerships with tools like Scrunch for SEO analysis](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=2296.0)
  - [Potential to expand into various AI-powered use cases beyond content creation](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=2642.0)

### Sales Process and Strategy

  - [Need to refine the sales process as the company grows and moves upmarket](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1200.0)
  - [Opportunity to implement more nurture touchpoints and leave-behinds](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=2940.0)
  - [Potential to leverage AI agents (like Vappy) in the sales process](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=2881.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Kyle to discuss Andrew's candidacy with Bridget and Tucker](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1076.0)
  - [Follow up with Andrew regarding next steps in the hiring process](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1081.0)
  - [Continue refining GrowthX's go-to-market strategy and product roadmap](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=1913.0)
  - [Explore opportunities to enhance the sales process with AI-powered tools and nurture sequences](https://fathom.video/share/Q1_fez5A7qBksGFUAPx_JFvxoMgt8PNt?tab=summary&timestamp=2940.0)

action_items.description: Prep for Aug board meeting; prioritize platform features/direction for next phase
Explore implementing inbound SDR agent w/ Vappy (similar to Andrew's suggestion)
Develop nurture strategy for early-stage prospects (e.g. Broccoli); incl. touch points, Marcelo involvement
Circle back w/ Bridget & Tucker re Andrew's interview; discuss next steps
